excerpt_en: 2024/11/09. It was my last day in Malta. In the morning I sat in on a meeting with a Japanese man who plans to do working holiday in Ireland; Mikola advised him, but as a wealthy entrepreneur his sense of reality is different, so I offered a bit of practical input. I learned that choosing who you consult matters. Then I visited a shop selling Japanese melon bread — they had turtle-shaped melon bread, so I took photos with the Masu turtle. After that we went to Golden Bay with the crew from a recent house party. We toasted with beer, did a short hike, and watched the sunset. I packed while others prepared a Japanese cooking party. I reflected on how this Malta trip happened because I joined international events in Ireland, and how fortunate it was to stay in Mikola’s great home for a week. At the airport lounge, I enjoyed food and drinks, but right before boarding I was told I had one extra bag and paid 60 euros. Malta was amazing despite the surprise fee, and now it’s time for Greece.
body_en:
2024/11/09

It’s Saturday, and today is my last day in Malta!

This morning, I had a meeting with a man who is a friend of my host Mikola’s Japanese friend.

He plans to do a working holiday in Ireland,

and wanted advice.

I knew I wasn’t the main adviser,

so I didn’t jump in too much, but

Mikola (who is 40) was advising him on finding work and housing.

Honestly, since Mikola is an entrepreneur with money,

his sense of reality is different,

and finding a job and housing isn’t that easy.

I had researched seriously for three months,

and I’m closer in age, so

I added a little input at the end.

Watching this, I learned that if you don’t choose the right person to consult,

the advice may not be meaningful.

After that,

I went to a shop that sells Japanese melon bread, which a model told me about yesterday.

They have turtle-shaped melon bread,

so as FOMUS, with a turtle mascot,

I really wanted to shoot it together!

So I walked about 20 minutes to the shop.

I’m not sure if the owner was Japanese,

but there were no Japanese people in the shop.

Still, I got great photos.

Later I posted a two-shot with Masu Turtle on Instagram,

and they mentioned me right away.

After that,

from a nearby bus stop, we went to Golden Bay, a Malta scenic spot.

It was with the group I met Tuesday and had a house party with Wednesday.

Golden Bay is a beach.

It wasn’t overly crowded,

just the right amount of people.

We started with a beer toast.

Beer on the beach is the best.

Mikola had a drone,

so while he filmed with two girls,

I finished my work.

*Replying to emails, writing blog posts

It’s not that I’m uninterested in drones —

I know too much about them,

so I no longer feel “wow, drones are amazing!”

*My parent is a pro

In a way, it was good to have time alone.

After that, a short hike.

About 20 minutes, up a mountain path.

This is the view of Golden Bay.

It’s best seen with the naked eye.

After that, they did more drone filming,

so I talked with the girls in turn.

Neither is working yet,

so my activities might sound confusing, but

I feel I conveyed, “This kind of path exists.”

I hope we can meet again somewhere in Japan or the world.

Before I knew it, it was evening,

so we watched the sunset and went home.

Then I packed my things,

while the other three prepared a Japanese cooking party.

I had a flight, so

I left early.

I talked with Mikola at the end, and

this Malta trip started because I met him,

and if I hadn’t shown up at international exchange events in Ireland,

this connection wouldn’t have happened.

For about a week,

I stayed at his place,

he cooked meals,

we had a home party,

so I didn’t spend money on food or lodging except when going out.

He’s an entrepreneur,

and the lifestyle was extremely stimulating.

His place had a great location and a big room,

and even though I slept on the sofa,

my body didn’t hurt at all.

He usually lives alone

and occasionally invites Japanese friends for a home party,

so he said it was really fun to have someone stay for a full week.

We both work for ourselves,

so our lifestyle is a bit unusual,

and it was a very free way of living.

I’m grateful that he invited me,

and I’m glad I could accept the invitation with my light footwork.

Thanks to that,

I had many encounters this week.

Of course, I don’t want it to end with just this one meeting,

and if there’s a chance, we’ll meet again somewhere in the world.

He now knows about the FOMUS brand,

so maybe we’ll connect through masu someday.

Anyway,

overall, it was an amazing Malta stay.

🔻Malta lounge

Fifteen minutes after returning from the beach,

I packed and got on a bus.

I didn’t have time to join the Japanese cooking party,

so I went to Malta International Airport.

I finished the procedures quickly,

and went to the Malta VIP lounge.

I have Priority Pass,

so I can use lounges as much as I want.

It’s become one of my recent pleasures.

The Malta lounge also had food and alcohol,

so I enjoyed the meal.

I like this time too.

Then it was time for the Greece flight.

As I reflected on what a good day it was,

I tried to board, but a staff member stopped me:

“You have one too many bags.”

“There will be an extra fee.”

Right before departure, I had to pay 60 euros.

I’d flown out of Dublin many times,

and it was the first time I was told that, so I was surprised.

I had no choice but to pay or I couldn’t board,

so I paid, but

it was unexpected after never having issues before.

Still, great trips come with accidents like this.

So,

despite the twists, Malta was a fun trip.

From here, the Greece chapter begins!!

What kind of encounters await?

I’m excited again!

excerpt_en: I arrived in Greece around 0:30, just 1.5 hours from Malta, and there was no immigration check. Since it was late and safety isn’t great, I stayed overnight in the airport (not a hotel). The first floor seats were full, but on the second floor I found a recessed gallery area where people were sleeping, so I slept there too, using my sandals and shoulder bag as a pillow and keeping my luggage between the wall and me. After about five hours, I headed into Athens. A bus dropped everyone off mid-route, so I took the subway and paid extra. I left my luggage at the hotel and decided to visit the Archaeological Museum first, buying Parthenon tickets at the same time. The museum was quiet right after opening. I explored while thinking about display methods and what could be useful for FOMUS. I had a kebab meal for about 6 euros, visited a free hill with a city view, checked in to the hotel, and finally slept.
body_en:
I arrived in Greece!

From Malta it’s about 1.5 hours,

so I arrived at 0:30.

There was no immigration check,

so I entered directly.

Arriving at this time,

it takes about an hour to get from the airport to central Athens,

and I knew safety isn’t great,

so I decided to sleep at the airport.

Not a hotel.

Real airport sleep.

So I looked for a place to sleep.

All the first-floor chairs were taken,

so there was nowhere to sleep.

I went up to the second floor and found a recessed gallery area like this!

And a few people were already sleeping there.

So,

I challenged myself to sleep there too!

This is my 4th time sleeping at an airport,

but my first time sleeping on the floor like this.

I lay down like this,

used my sandals in a bag and my shoulder bag as a pillow,

and placed all my luggage between the wall and me for full security.

I couldn’t fall asleep right away,

so I watched a bit of YouTube, then slept.

I rested for about five hours there.

Then around 6:30,

I headed into Athens.

After checking,

there was a bus that goes near my hotel,

so I took it, but

for some reason, everyone was made to get off midway.

I had no choice, so I took the subway to the station.

Of course I paid extra for that.

After riding a packed subway,

I made it to the nearest area.

I immediately left my luggage at the hotel.

I had no plans today,

so I sat in the hotel lobby and decided where to go.

I wanted to visit the Parthenon in perfect condition on day two,

so I went to the Archaeological Museum instead.

At the same time,

I bought Parthenon tickets too.

So I went to the museum, which opens at 8:30.

Since it was right after opening,

there weren’t many people.

Basically, I was thinking about:

“What’s a good way to display things?”

“Is there anything I can use for FOMUS?”

People who like history would probably enjoy this more,

but I’m not great at it, and I thought

if I were more interested, I could dive deeper...

but I didn’t have that feeling. Lol

Still, I enjoyed it!

Then I went to find lunch.

I found a kebab place,

and thought about takeout,

but it looked like I could eat in,

so I went in.

The kebab I know is the spinning one you slice off,

but this was different.

Bread, meat, grilled tomato, and onion.

About 6 euros.

Well, fine. Lol

The food was tasty!

After that, I went to a free hill.

From there I could see all of Athens.

It was beautiful enough from here,

but I’m sure the view from the Parthenon is different.

I’m going tomorrow, so I’ll look forward to it.

After that, I walked around Athens a bit,

and checked into the hotel.

Honestly, since the last day in Malta,

I hadn’t really slept, so I was exhausted

*Plus the hike

*Active from yesterday morning to tonight

I lay down for a bit, but

ended up falling asleep.

When I woke up, it was 1 AM.

There was nothing to do, so

I went back to sleep.

Tomorrow I have a Parthenon reservation at 8 AM,

so as long as I make that, it’s fine.

title_en: Parthenon Temple / Masu Photo in Greece
excerpt_en: After more than 12 hours of sleep, I headed to the Parthenon right at 8 a.m. There were a few people already, but the morning crowd was small. I skipped the other spots and went straight to the main temple. The weather was perfect, and I shot with the Masu turtle, though it broke after five minutes. I still got some videos for social media. Then I rushed to the meeting spot and did two Masu Photo shoots, one with a Greek model and one with a Russian-Greek model. I realized my English has improved a lot compared to last year in Malaysia. Athens isn’t cheap, so I kept meals minimal. At night I finally went to a bar, but joining Greek conversations was hard. Still, two shoots at the Parthenon made the day a big win.
body_en:
After more than 12 hours of sleep,

I completely cleared my sleep debt,

and got to the Parthenon entrance right at 8 a.m.

Some people were already waiting,

but since it was morning, the crowd was still small.

I saved the other spots for later

and headed straight to the main Parthenon.

*The Parthenon is a grand ancient Greek temple in Athens, Greece, dedicated to the goddess Athena, the protector of the city. It was built from 447 to 432 BCE, and its pediments and metopes are decorated with sculptures depicting myths and wars.

The Parthenon is known as a masterpiece of classical Greek architecture. With its distinctive Doric columns and perfect proportions, it holds an extremely important place in architectural history. The building has subtle curves that make it look more beautiful to the eye. It’s also said that a 12-meter statue of Athena once stood inside.

Over its long history, the temple was used as a Christian church and an Islamic mosque, but it was damaged by wars and explosions, and today it remains as ruins.

This is the Parthenon.

The weather was perfect,

ideal for shooting!

So I shot with the Masu turtle.

But the turtle’s face had been damaged,

it was wobbly, and I didn’t have any glue,

so it only held together for the first five minutes.

After that, it became difficult to shoot with the Masu turtle.

Still, I managed to take some videos for social media.

Afterward,

I went around other spots at the Parthenon

and spent about two hours there.

I was getting hungry,

so I started looking for good food,

when I got a message.

“I’m already at the meeting spot. Where are you?”

Huh?

Today’s Masu Photo was supposed to be

from 10:30,

so I thought I still had 30 minutes,

but it actually started at 10:00...

Totally my mistake.

*Mistakes like this are system errors for me,

so to make sure it never happens again,

I’ll put it in Google Calendar as soon as a booking is set.

Still, the meeting spot was about a 10-minute walk from the temple, so I moved quickly

and met the Masu Photo model!

She was Greek,

knew the area well,

and taught me a bunch of spots.

We chatted and took photos for about an hour,

and it turned into a great Masu Photo shoot!

What I felt again was

that I’m now able to communicate in English,

which brought me much closer to the model.

For me compared to a year ago,

this is a huge change,

and it’s worlds apart from the Masu Photo shoot

I did in Malaysia last October.

Because I tasted failure then,

I quickly decided to study abroad,

spent three months each in the Philippines and Ireland,

and kept adding Masu Photo shoots in my free time

to increase my chances to speak English.

Now the results are really showing.

This is big progress,

and I can feel it myself,

so it gives me confidence.

Still, I’m not there yet,

so I’ll keep training my English.

After the shoot,

I went for brunch and back to the hotel.

That said,

Athens isn’t as cheap as you might think.

I don’t want to waste money,

so I walked around and

ended up choosing something like

“pizza bread x bolognese.”

I forgot to take a photo, but it was 4.3 euros.

Not too bad.

Since it’s just me,

I keep lodging and food costs

as low as possible.

🔻Masu Photo #2

And then,

the second Masu Photo shoot of the day!

She was Russian,

with parents from Russia and Greece.

She said she was interested in Japanese traditions and culture,

so she joined this time.

To be honest,

it takes a lot of courage to apply to a mysterious project like this.

Because it’s basically a call saying,

“Would you like to join a project where a foreign photographer

takes photos together with a wooden box from Japanese traditional craft?”

She cleared that hurdle,

and since I personally check each person,

the models who show up for Masu Photo

are truly rare!!

That’s why

I want to take each person seriously,

and I don’t want it to end as a one-time thing.

So,

Masu Photo shooting.

This time the main focus was交流 (interaction),

and the photo shoot was secondary.

Even so, we still get great photos.

As I talked with her,

I learned she was 18 and not working yet.

Honestly I thought she was about 22,

but I was totally wrong!

She had come with her grandmother,

though she wasn’t there during the shoot,

and since the shoot ended safely,

I tried to send her home with her grandmother,

but she said, “My grandma went home already.”

I see!

So I wondered if she could get home on her own,

but she didn’t seem in a hurry to leave.

After the shoot,

I was planning to explore Athens alone,

but for some reason the model decided to come with me. lol

I was happy because I had someone to talk to,

but I also felt like keeping an 18-year-old around late

might worry her parents,

but she said it was fine,

and we ended up having dinner together. lol

The shoot started at 15:30,

and it finished after 16:00,

but we didn’t split up until 18:30.

She said it would take about 30 minutes by transport

from the shooting spot,

and she didn’t know how to buy a ticket,

so I went with her until she got on the tram.

I’m glad I could see her off safely!

Then I went back to the hotel to drop off my camera,

and decided to go out for a drink.

Yesterday I slept from 3 a.m. and

woke up at 7 a.m.,

so this was somehow my first nighttime! lol

That said, I didn’t know which bar was good, so I wandered around checking Google ratings.

I ended up in some random bar

and just had one beer.

No real conversations.

There were other customers at the bar,

but they were speaking Greek,

and it felt like a high hurdle to jump into that.

I got the impression that

it’s hard unless you go to events like exchange meetups.

Originally I was supposed to be introduced to a Greek person,

but he got sick and couldn’t meet me, so I had zero connections.

There were no Japanese people walking around either.

Still, I went to the Parthenon

and got two Masu Photo shoots done,

so work-wise it was a very productive day!

Tomorrow is totally free,

and I’m going to enjoy it!

excerpt_en: I had plans for a shoot and to meet a friend, but both got canceled, leaving me with no schedule. I also switched accommodations to a dorm for about 5,000 yen for two nights. With nothing to do until evening, I focused on building FOMUS. At night I joined an international exchange event I found on the meetup app where I’m an organizer in Dublin. It was also held in Athens. It grew to around ten people. I talked with the Cypriot organizer and shared about my work, but after four months abroad I know these meetups rarely lead to FOMUS business because local business owners don’t attend. Still, I keep showing up. We wrapped around 10 p.m., and I was the only Asian there.
body_en:
I had plans for a shoot and to meet a friend,

but both got canceled,

so my day ended up empty.

I also changed accommodations at this timing.

I moved to a hostel for about 5,000 yen for two nights.

A dormitory.

With nothing scheduled until night,

I just kept building the FOMUS business.

At night, I joined an international exchange event I found on the meetup app where I’m an organizer in Dublin.

Following Estonia,

it was also held in Athens.

When I got there,

only about three people had arrived,

but it gradually increased to around ten.

*For some reason I only took a group photo in the first half of the event.

The organizer was from Cyprus,

and when I told him I was heading to Cyprus after Greece,

he gave me a bunch of tips.

We also talked about my work.

My goal is to communicate with all kinds of people.

Some people speak insanely fast and with a bit of an accent,

so they can be hard to understand.

I told them I’m a photographer

and that I run the Masu Photo project,

but after traveling abroad for more than four months, I’ve learned that

these kinds of international exchange meetups

almost never connect to FOMUS business.

That’s because local business owners

don’t attend events from meetup apps.

This was also true in Ireland.

Still, the chance isn’t zero,

so I keep participating.

I just have to keep stepping up to the plate.

We chatted until around 10 p.m. and then broke up.

As expected,

there were no Japanese people,

and actually no other Asians besides me. lol

title_en: I Don’t Trust People Right Away
excerpt_en: Today I had multiple online meetings. The first was about a house-share plan in Ireland with four Japanese members, but suddenly one person had already secured their own place and another decided to return to Japan in January. If they’d said that earlier, I could have used my time differently. I wasn’t angry, but the late notice was disappointing, and the plan collapsed. Then I got messages from the organizer I met at an international exchange event, asking to join a shoot or go find models. That’s not my style, so I ignored him. He later left a very sexual comment on my story, so I blocked him immediately. I don’t build trust after one meeting, and I cut off people fast if I see harmful behavior. Later I had a meeting with my Japanese members about the FOMUS subscription and felt energized by their support. This trip isn’t a glamorous vacation — it’s a grind — but I’ll keep stepping up to the plate and push forward on the path I chose.
body_en:
Today I had multiple online meetings.

The first was about a house.

Actually, a plan had been moving forward for four Japanese members to live together in Ireland from December,

and to host events there if possible.

So this was the timing to look for a place,

and I hoped at least one of us would find a property.

I was the one who proposed it,

so of course I’d been looking, found a place,

and today was the meeting to discuss it.

I’m in Greece right now,

so it was an online call.

My listening has improved a bit, so I managed to understand

and we decided to move forward to a viewing.

I’m not in Ireland this week,

so I told the group I’d ask the others to handle the viewing,

and then suddenly,

one person said they had already secured a place on their own,

and another said they were going back in January, so they were out.

Wait, wait,

wasn’t the plan to live together as four?

And more importantly,

if you told me earlier,

I could have used my time searching for a house

for my own business or my own place...

They’re friends,

and it’s not a business relationship,

so I’m not mad,

but being told after the fact was sad.

And the other person who was searching with me

now has to find a place on their own, which I feel bad about.

Being selfish and reporting after the fact

causes a chain reaction of inconvenience for others,

so I’m using this as a negative example.

So the Ireland house-share plan collapsed.

That means I now have to find a place to live alone.

Well, I’ll make it work.

🔻Don’t take me lightly

More bad things happened.

I posted Masu Photos from a model shoot the other day,

and I got a message from

the organizer of the international exchange event I met yesterday.

I wondered what it was,

and he said, “If you’re shooting today, can I join you?”

“If not, want to go into town and look for models together?”

I don’t do street pickup,

and I don’t go out looking for models with a Masu in hand,

because it’s far from FOMUS’s理念 (principles).

On top of that, he kept inviting me to go drinking.

I ignored all of it.

Because even if I met him again,

I wouldn’t gain anything useful,

and honestly, since we only met at an exchange event,

he wasn’t someone I wanted a strong connection with.

I have a pretty sensitive filter on people.

That’s because over the past 5–6 years,

I’ve met a lot of people

and experienced betrayal, jealousy, and envy.

I don’t trust someone after just one meeting.

Then, while I was ignoring him,

he left a comment on the Masu Photo story.

And it was a very sexual, depressing comment.

Is it common for a male organizer in his late 30s to be like that?

I don’t need connections like this,

so I blocked him immediately.

I’ll never meet him again.

If I see even a hint of behavior

that could harm me or the people around me,

I cut it off immediately.

Being fast with that judgment is one of my strengths.

This isn’t really a negative example —

it’s probably just bad luck.

🔻Meeting with Japanese members

After all that,

I had a meeting with my Japanese members.

I hadn’t spoken Japanese in a while,

and I asked for input on the FOMUS subscription that’s about to start.

Just to get their opinions.

I don’t usually show my worries,

though I probably should,

but there’s some useless pride inside me,

so I’m going to drop it fast.

Then a friend suddenly called me,

and it was fun to talk for a bit.

(Sentokun and Shibata-san)

They find my activities interesting,

and they’re building their own community in Japan.

That’s why I’m building an overseas community

with FOMUS and Massu as the hub,

and doing the best I can in each environment.

The support from people in Japan

is definitely reaching me,

and it makes me feel fired up to keep going!

Anyway,

my expedition isn’t just tourism,

and it’s far from a glittering overseas stay.

This kind of stuff goes on endlessly,

and I can’t write it all down,

and by the time I write this,

I’m already reframing the bad things positively

and forgetting the negatives...

It’s a constant cycle like that.

Just like my work in Japan,

in business, things almost never go as planned.

My batting average is low,

so I have to keep stepping up to the plate

and increase the number of tries, or I’ll never succeed.

There are plenty of easier paths,

but this is the path I chose,

so I’m going to keep pushing forward

until it becomes the right answer.

excerpt_en: Today was my last day in Greece, and I had a morning Masu Photo shoot with Hermione. She works in finance at a supermarket HQ and speaks five languages, even though she’s never left Greece. After the shoot I decided to buy a small suitcase. Carrying two bags means extra airline fees, and I’ll need to transport about 80 masu boxes for a Japanese embassy event back in Dublin. I bargained the suitcase down to 20 euros and bought it. I kept meals super cheap: 4 euros for biryani and 2.5 euros for kebab. Then I created a Masu Photo model group. It’s a community for past and future models to connect; I want these relationships to last, not be one-off. I also reflected on my Greece trip: no local connections, but two shoots, an international meetup, and a Parthenon visit made it worthwhile. Next stop: Cyprus.
body_en:
So,

my stay in Greece is already on its final day.

And this morning I had a Masu Photo shoot.

The model was Hermione.

The hill I climbed on my first day in Athens

had an amazing view (you can see the Parthenon),

so we met at the station

and walked there while talking.

She told me she manages finance at the headquarters of a supermarket.

I assume she’s older than me.

*Sorry if I’m wrong.

What surprised me in our conversation was

that she speaks five languages.

Yet she has never left Greece!

She said she just likes studying.

I can barely handle two languages,

Japanese and English,

so speaking five is incredible.

*The Parthenon is in the background 🏛️

Once again, shooting abroad is fun.

We shot for about an hour and then said goodbye!

I thought about exploring a bit,

but there’s always work to do for FOMUS,

so I went back to the hotel.

Before that, I happened to find a shop

selling suitcases.

Considering what’s ahead,

I thought, “I might actually need one.”

Up to now, for smaller trips,

I’ve been moving with a backpack

and a tote bag,

but the tote is stuffed,

the backpack is stuffed,

and my shoulders are killing me.

On the flight from Malta to Greece,

I learned that carrying two bags onboard

means an extra fee,

and I ended up paying 60 euros,

so I want to avoid that on the Greece → Cyprus flight too.

Apparently it’s 24 euros if you add baggage online,

so I’ve already accepted that cost,

and if I get a small suitcase, it should qualify.

If I’m paying the same fee anyway,

I’d rather move more comfortably with a suitcase.

Also, the first job after returning to Dublin

is an event at the Japanese Embassy in Ireland,

and I’ll need to bring about 80 masu boxes.

Carrying that with just a backpack and tote is tough.

Plus, I currently have about 150 masu boxes in Dublin,

and my travel suitcase has broken wheels, so it’s hard to move them.

For all these reasons,

I decided I should buy a small suitcase.

There were two suitcase shops, and after negotiating,

I got the price down to 20 euros and bought one.

I only learned during this trip

that bringing two bags means extra airline fees,

so it adds to the ticket cost,

and it was more expensive than I expected,

so I went ultra-frugal on food in Greece. lol

Lunch was biryani, which I loved yesterday.

4 euros.

Dinner was kebab, which I also loved yesterday.

2.5 euros.

Total 6.5 euros.

About 1,200 yen for both lunch and dinner.

That’s insanely cheap for Europe! lol

In Japan you could easily do both meals for under 1,000 yen,

but with the yen weak right now,

keeping it under 1,000 yen feels like amazing value.

When it’s just me, I’m really stingy with food.

Anyway,

I worked at the hotel until around 8 p.m. after checkout.

Now here’s the main topic.

🔻I created a Masu Photo model group

The title says it all,

I created a group specifically for Masu Photo models.

The concept of FOMUS is:

Connecting people through masu

and creating an environment that generates new value.

Up to now, it was just me connecting everyone,

but at this timing I created a group.

I did consider the FOMUS subscription community

that will start soon, but

I realized there’s no real benefit for models

to pay money to join a community,

so instead I created a place where Masu Photo models can connect with each other.

Even if I charged money

and made a Masu Photo model group,

we couldn’t create value with this group right away,

and beyond交流 (interaction),

I still don’t even know what the value is yet.

But if I build the community now,

future Masu Photo models will join,

and the number of people will slowly increase.

The models in this group

aren’t necessarily pro models,

and they’re not necessarily business owners either,

so they aren’t specialized in creating things.

But what they all have in common is:

They held a FOMUS masu,

and took Masu Photos with Massu.

This connection can’t be bought with money.

They saw a random Instagram ad

and raised their hands themselves,

which means they have

an unusually strong appetite for challenges and curiosity.

And I want to support them if they decide to take on something,

and when FOMUS grows bigger,

I want to create more interesting things with these models

as part of the FOMUS members.

Some of them have incredible talents,

and someday I might collaborate with them in some form.

There’s another reason I started this group,

and to be honest it’s the more serious issue:

the relationships with models I shot six months ago

are gradually fading.

After meeting them,

I don’t want it to be just a one-time thing.

So instead of only FOMUS and me being connected,

if people with the same common point become friends,

I think they’ll feel,

“I’m glad I joined Masu Photo.”

As of 2024/11/14,

I’m still searching for what form this will take,

but whether it’s in 3 years, 5 years, or 10 years,

I’ll go find the day when I’m glad I made this group.

And more than anything,

what I need to go all-in on is

the FOMUS subscription.

It’s not an online salon,

so I won’t really be teaching anything,

but I want to build an environment where

being in it is fun,

you hear stories you can’t usually hear,

and you can enjoy my experiences and FOMUS’s English services,

so people feel that being involved with FOMUS is exciting!

🔻Looking back on the Greece expedition

And with that, the Greece expedition is over.

The person I originally planned to meet

got sick and I couldn’t see them,

so I had zero connections there,

and there wasn’t a single person I knew locally,

so I didn’t have any chances to go out with someone.

Still,

meeting Masu Photo models

and communicating in English at the international exchange event

were very meaningful,

and I finally got to see the Parthenon,

which I’d wanted to visit at least once,

so it was great.

This Greece trip also ended as just “sowing seeds,”

and I didn’t make any money,

but it was a valuable learning experience!

I’m officially allowed to earn money in Ireland,

so I’ll make sure to use this experience there!

And with that, I’m off to Cyprus!!

My flight this time was at 5 a.m.,

so I got to the airport at 10 p.m. — seven hours before —

and even finished baggage check,

but the lounge was closed.

*I knew it.

Next time, I’ll take a flight during lounge hours!

excerpt_en: Today I arrived in Cyprus and will stay about four days in the southern city of Paphos. After landing I took a bus toward the city, but Google Maps doesn’t support buses in Cyprus, so I rode to a nearby stop and then walked about 30 minutes. Since it was only 8:30 a.m. and I couldn’t leave my bags, I had breakfast at a cafe. The chocolate croissant was amazing. After checking in around noon, I walked 45 minutes to the Paphos Archaeological Park, stopping for a Cyprus kebab at a mall. The park is huge, with powerful mosaics, and then I visited Paphos Castle by the harbor. I learned the castle’s seaside location was strategic for defense and trade. On the way back I bought lemon sherbet and later stocked the fridge with local beer. I was exhausted after only an hour of sleep, so I went to bed early.
body_en:
From today, I’m in Cyprus.

I plan to stay about four days in Paphos, a southern city.

After arriving, I took a bus into town.

Unfortunately, Google Maps

doesn’t support buses in Cyprus,

so I rode to a nearby stop,

and then walked about 30 minutes.

Getting a suitcase helped a lot,

so it wasn’t too tough.

When I arrived at my accommodation,

it was still 8:30 a.m., so I couldn’t leave my bags,

so I had breakfast at a nearby cafe.

The chocolate croissant

was insanely good!!

I sat outside,

the weather was great, and I worked there.

For some reason,

there were a lot of students passing by,

so maybe it was some kind of class.

Then around noon,

I checked in at the hotel

and put my bags away.

The forecast said today was the only good weather,

so I headed to Paphos Archaeological Park.

It was a 45-minute walk.

On the way there was a huge mall,

so I had lunch there.

I ate a Cyprus kebab.

About 8 euros!

Expensive, but delicious,

and super filling!

These kebabs are different from the ones Japanese people know,

and they’re served on the skewer.

I ate a lot of them in Greece too.

And then, to the ruins.

🔻World Heritage

I went to Paphos Archaeological Park, a World Heritage site.

Paphos Archaeological Park (Αρχαιολογικός Χώρος Νέας Πάφου) is a vast open-air museum located in the Kato Paphos area of Paphos City in southwest Cyprus. The park contains ruins from antiquity through the medieval period, and it was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980.

Main highlights

  • House of Dionysus: A villa built in the late 2nd century. Within its roughly 2,000-square-meter grounds, about 556 square meters of mosaic floors remain. The mosaics depict scenes from Greek mythology, grape harvests, hunting, and more, and they are considered highly artistic.

-

The mosaics are incredibly powerful in person,

but somehow photos don’t capture it well. lol

I walked around the park for about an hour.

This place is huge!

Then I went to Paphos Castle,

outside the park.

Paphos Castle

  • A medieval castle built in the 13th century next to Paphos Harbor. It was also used during the Ottoman period.
  • From the top of the castle, you can overlook the harbor and sea, and it’s also popular for its sunsets.

I hadn’t really seen a castle by the sea before,

so I wondered why,

and looked it up.

The reason Paphos Castle is right by the sea is its strategic location. The main reasons are:

1. For defense

  • By being on the coastline, the castle could be used as a defensive base against attacks from the sea.
  • In medieval times, coastal castles functioned as key points to protect ports and cities. Paphos Castle was designed to defend the harbor and surrounding area.

2. Trade and harbor control

  • Paphos Harbor was an important trade hub from antiquity through the medieval period. A castle by the sea could manage the harbor and control maritime traffic and trade.
  • Because it overlooked the harbor, it could monitor ships coming and going, collect taxes, and prevent smuggling.

3. A key point on Mediterranean routes

  • Paphos is located at the center of the Mediterranean, an important point on sea trade routes connecting Europe, Asia, and Africa.
  • A seaside castle protected ships on these routes and also served as a defense against pirates.

4. Logistics and transport

  • Being by the sea made it easier to supply and transport military materials. Compared to inland castles, maintenance and logistics were more efficient.

5. Symbolic role

  • Seaside castles also symbolized the authority of a city or region. For ships and foreign merchants, they demonstrated the power of the area.

Paphos Castle was built in the 13th century by the Lusignan dynasty and used under Ottoman rule as well, and it still carries historical value today. Its seaside location made it not only a defensive and administrative base, but also a landmark symbolizing Paphos’s importance.

That’s what GPT told me.

So it was a trade hub too!

Anyway, that’s how it was.

On the way back, I saw ice cream for sale,

so I bought a lemon sherbet.

It was super tasty!

Then I walked about 40 minutes back to the hotel.

In Cyprus I’m staying three nights with no hotel change,

so I have a fridge,

and I stocked up on local alcohol at the supermarket.

Going to a pub alone

isn’t that fun. lol

But I still wanted to check the beer,

so I bought some.

Can you tell which ones are Cyprus beer and which ones are Greek? lol

The ones on both ends are Cyprus beers.

KEO is a lager,

and I liked it personally!

You can drink it for 1.7 euros,

so it’s not that different from Japan!

With only about one hour of sleep,

I was active from morning to night,

so I was pretty tired and went to bed early!

excerpt_en: Day 2 in Cyprus. I did a morning Masu Photo shoot with Athina, a Cypriot basketball coach who likes Japanese culture and anime. Even though the forecast was rain, it turned sunny, and that meant I completed Masu Photo shoots in all Mediterranean countries this time: Malta, Greece, and Cyprus. Recruiting models is still tough with short stays and only Instagram ads, so I want to create a situation where five people gather in a day on the next expedition and build a monetization model. After the shoot I walked 20 minutes to the Tombs of the Kings, a UNESCO World Heritage site, and explored the underground tombs, pigeons, and a huge agama lizard. In the evening I had a FOMUS meeting over a massive kebab, and ended the day with two days left in Cyprus, hoping the rain lets me visit a winery.
body_en:
Day 2 in Cyprus.

This morning,

Masu Photo shooting!

The model was Athina.

She’s from Cyprus.

She also coaches basketball,

and she had a game after the shoot.

She’s never been to Japan,

but she likes the culture and anime,

so she applied when she heard a Japanese photographer was coming.

Being Japanese often works as an advantage abroad.

In about an hour, she also told me recommended spots,

and it turned into a really fulfilling shoot!

The weather was supposed to be rainy,

but it turned sunny!

That means during this Mediterranean expedition,

I did Masu Photo shoots in every country:

Malta, Greece, and Cyprus.

Attracting people is really tough,

and honestly I want to shoot about 10 people in each country,

but my stays are short,

it’s hard to align schedules with local models,

and with only Instagram ads,

it’s hard to fully communicate the purpose.

For the next expedition,

I want to clear this somehow

and create a situation where about five people gather in a day.

Also, Masu Photo is a promotion,

not a project that directly makes money,

so I need to build a business model

that monetizes this too.

There are always mountains of challenges...

🔻To the Tombs of the Kings

After the shoot,

the next place I went was

the Tombs of the Kings,

about a 20-minute walk away.

The Tombs of the Kings (Tombs of the Kings / Τάφοι των Βασιλέων)** are

a grand underground necropolis in Paphos, Cyprus, and a UNESCO World Heritage site.

The site features designs influenced by ancient Egypt, Greece, and Rome,

and dates from the 4th century BCE to the 3rd century CE.

Features of the Tombs of the Kings

  1. Origin of the name
  • Royalty weren’t actually buried here; high-ranking officials and wealthy citizens were. The name “Tombs of the Kings” comes from the grand scale and lavish architecture.
  1. Architecture
  • The tombs are carved out of rock and spread underground.
  • Many are designed around courtyards with colonnades, showing influences from ancient Egyptian and Greek architecture.
  1. Decorations and artifacts
  • It’s believed the interiors were once decorated with murals and carvings, but almost none remain today.
  • Many relics and offerings were left in the tombs, providing clues about ancient burial rituals and lifestyles.
  1. Unique burial practices
  • Bodies were placed in stone sarcophagi or wall niches. Many tombs were used by families, so there are also large shared tombs.

Historical background

  • This necropolis was used during the Hellenistic period (323–31 BCE) and the Roman period (31 BCE–395 CE).
  • It wasn’t just a burial site; it was designed as a “home” where the dead could rest eternally, reflecting beliefs about life and death at the time.

Highlights

  1. Massive colonnades
  • Some of the larger tombs have spacious courtyards with Doric columns, leaving a strong impression.
  1. Underground scale
  • The interiors feel like a labyrinth, and you can explore them like ruins on an adventure.
  1. Close to the sea
  • The tombs are along the coast, so you can also enjoy beautiful Mediterranean views.

There weren’t any single huge highlights,

but walking around here was fun.

Down underground, pigeons had built nests,

so I saw scenery you don’t usually see,

and I also saw a pretty big lizard.

Apparently it was an agama lizard.

Much bigger than lizards in Japan,

and it was the most exciting part of the Tombs! lol

🔻Japan meeting

Then in the evening,

I had a FOMUS-related meeting.

While looking for dinner, I found a kebab place

with a cafe terrace,

so I ate kebab there and then did the meeting.

Can you tell how big it is? lol

It was huge!

This was 6.5 euros.

That’s about 1,100 yen. lol

I really want to keep it under 5 euros,

but that would mean cooking for myself. lol

Once I’m back in Ireland I’ll be cooking,

so during the Mediterranean trip, I’ll eat out! lol

And just like that,

Day 2 in Cyprus is over!

Two days left!

And the forecast says the rest will be rainy.

If it doesn’t rain,

I really want to make it to a winery.

Let’s see how it goes! lol

title_en: What You Can Do When You Have Nothing to Lose
excerpt_en: Day 3 in Cyprus. With rain in the forecast and most shops closed on Sunday, I took a walk to a nearby museum. I’m not only interested in exhibits but also how they’re arranged. After about 30 minutes I finished and worked at the hotel. Traveling alone gave me time to rethink FOMUS’s overseas strategy. Just placing masu in restaurants isn’t expansion, and selling a logo-only masu as a souvenir is tough. Tourists buy postcards, stickers, magnets, glasses, and character goods. So I’m thinking of a branded character design and region-limited souvenirs. I reached out to a top designer but was rejected because FOMUS uses AI, which some creators avoid. AI is essential for me, so I’ll keep searching for a designer who can create a global character, because the new brand will focus on souvenirs for foreign travelers.
body_en:
Day 3 in Cyprus.

It was originally forecast to rain,

and since it was Sunday and everything was closed,

I took a walk to a nearby museum.

I’m interested not only in the exhibits,

but also in the way they’re arranged,

so I was thinking, “Ah, this is how they stage it,”

“So this kind of display method exists too,”

which might be a slightly different way of looking at it.

I finished in about 30 minutes,

and then just worked at the hotel.

I’ve been around Malta, Greece, and Cyprus,

and after Malta

I had a lot of time alone,

so I was able to think slowly about my business and activities.

When you run your own business,

the anxiety never really goes away.

And I’ve been thinking nonstop about

how to expand the FOMUS brand overseas,

and moving on it for the past six months,

but just getting restaurants to place masu

doesn’t really count as expansion.

Of course, it would be amazing to have our own shop.

But there are budget issues, and

selling masu to people who don’t even know what masu are

is really difficult.

You don’t see masu souvenirs overseas.

But that’s exactly why

I think there’s a chance.

If we can leave an impact in one country or region,

I believe it can spread from there.

What I’m aiming for is

souvenirs.

Tourists always buy some kind of souvenir.

When I was abroad and even when I was in Japan,

I was always watching

what tourists were paying attention to.

Souvenirs people buy overseas are

postcards, stickers,

fridge magnets, glasses, and character goods.

And I realized (too late)

that the FOMUS-branded masu

isn’t really a design that works as a souvenir.

That’s because

it has the brand logo and name on it.

As a communication tool, the FOMUS masu is great,

but for someone who doesn’t know the brand,

it’s just a box with a random logo.

That won’t sell as a souvenir.

In that case,

putting “I LOVE JAPAN” on it would sell better.

So I thought about it.

How can masu become a standard souvenir item?

It has to be a character.

But a random mascot isn’t enough —

it needs to be a properly branded character design.

And I’m planning to do it with a strategy of

only being able to buy it at specific tourist locations.

That means we need an eye-catching,

high-quality character

and strong brand imaging.

I’ve been thinking about that for about a week,

and I came up with an idea

and immediately put it into words.

That means I need a designer for it.

I wanted to ask a character designer who left an impact on the world,

so I sent them a message right away,

but after pushing three times,

I was ultimately turned down.

The reason was that with FOMUS, we’re moving projects

using AI and AI art.

In today’s era, AI is growing fast,

and illustrators and designers are losing jobs.

That means some creators find it hard to work

with businesses using AI like FOMUS.

For me,

I never expected that reason for a rejection.

AI will only grow more from here,

and I don’t have the option to not use it.

If I got rejected for that reason,

I just have to find the next person.

Still, it was a good learning experience.

To be honest, I want to ask a designer

who has created character art that works globally.

That’s because the new brand I plan to launch

will specialize in souvenirs for foreign travelers.

If it becomes popular overseas,

it will be wanted in Japan too,

so I want to make sure it expands abroad first.

So I’ll keep looking for a designer

who can draw the new FOMUS character!

title_en: I Need to Find Partners Around the World
excerpt_en: 2024/11/18. Day 4 in Cyprus. With no plans to meet anyone, I walked around Paphos and studied what tourists buy. The new direction for FOMUS is positioning masu as souvenirs with eye-catching character designs and “only here” exclusivity, potentially with local partners in each country. Postcards still sell well, and so do local lucky charms. I realized I hadn’t studied Irish souvenir shops enough, so I’ll do that once I’m back in Dublin. To build a global masu souvenir brand, I need partners, funding, and top-tier design. The Mediterranean trip ended with many lessons, while projects in Japan and Ireland faced delays. The Japanese embassy event in Ireland is on the 21st, and I want to leave an impact. I also decided to focus on Irish whiskey and Irish music collaborations after that. There’s no time to waste — I have to push forward and capture every opportunity.
body_en:
2024/11/18

Day 4 in Cyprus.

Unfortunately I didn’t have plans to meet anyone,

so I walked around Paphos on my own.

When I have time alone,

walking while thinking is a great way for me

to organize ideas.

So I walked about 40 minutes from the hotel

to the Paphos Castle area I also visited on the first day.

This is a tourist spot,

so even though it was my second time, I looked at souvenir shops

and kept watching what customers were doing

and what kinds of people they were.

The new direction I’m thinking about for FOMUS

is positioning masu purely as souvenirs.

Tourists everywhere buy souvenirs,

so I plan to sell masu with eye-catching character designs.

And I’ll add the element of

“you can only buy it here.”

It’s not finalized yet, but

first I’d like to commission a Japanese professional

to create the character concept,

and then in other countries and regions,

I plan to partner with locals

to expand it.

For that,

I need to visit tourist spots around the world,

look at souvenir shops, and carefully research what people buy.

To become the world’s only masu souvenir brand,

I have to force my way into a space with strong competition.

That’s why I also have to differentiate.

To be honest,

I kind of want to be edgy enough to say

“You can only buy it with Bitcoin or Ethereum.” lol

If there’s a system that lets me sell it 5% cheaper

than paying in yen or euros, I want to implement it.

So I spent a lot of time looking around shops.

🔻Postcards are strong

While looking around,

the demand for postcards is still big.

Even though they’re just postcards,

there are so many designs and people really think about what to choose.

And the price isn’t that high.

That makes them easy to buy.

Then there are lucky charms.

These are sold at all kinds of stores.

They aren’t branded, but

they’re rooted in the region and they sell.

Just like buying a Merlion in Singapore,

I realized we need to create items

you can only get there (and become memories).

To be honest,

I spent three months in Ireland, but

I don’t think I really looked at souvenirs that carefully.

I was spending time on language school and organizing events,

so I didn’t look at souvenirs much.

That’s why from tomorrow I’ll be back in Ireland,

planning to spend at least about two months in Dublin,

and I want to thoroughly check souvenir shops

and what tourists in Dublin are buying.

Based on that,

FOMUS is planning to launch a new brand,

and we might also create low-priced content besides masu.

I’m thinking about all that, but

none of it can start unless I find partners around the world

to do it together.

We say we’re the world’s only masu brand,

but the reality is we don’t make enough sales yet

to open a shop with only masu.

And overseas, rent is expensive.

So first,

I want to rent a section in someone else’s shop,

sell there, and once sales are solid,

expand the sales channels from there.

That means partners and fundraising,

and overwhelming quality in masu and design.

I have to do all of it.

When launching a new brand

There’s no issue with the quality of the masu,

so I need to find an artist

who can draw the “MASUKAME” brand logo.

*New brand name (tentative)

And I need to raise money.

And I also need to find partners.

Finding partners will be very hard, but in the last three months,

I’ve learned that people like that aren’t at Dublin’s international exchange events,

so I’ll keep running my own events,

and I’ll also jump into local walking events and marathons

to increase chances to connect with local people (business owners).

🔻So much to do

And with that, the Mediterranean expedition is over.

It was a short two weeks, but

I met a lot of people in Malta, Greece, and Cyprus,

did Masu Photo shoots,

connected with people, and got business hints.

On the other hand, in Japan and Ireland,

projects I was moving forward didn’t go as planned,

and I was stressing out.

The Sagishima project that FOMUS plans to produce was going to do ceiling art,

but we tried to get subsidies and couldn’t get approval,

and also the container ceiling wasn’t safe enough for ceiling art,

so it’s on pause.

We have to think of alternatives.

Also in Ireland,

I had created a group to rent a house together, but two people dropped,

so I had to suddenly find a house,

do online viewings, and decide on a place...

*Viewing a Dublin room from Cyprus! lol

In these two weeks,

all kinds of things happened in different countries.

And on the 21st,

I’ve been invited to an event at the Japanese Embassy in Ireland,

where I’ll serve sake using FOMUS masu.

This is right after I return to Dublin,

so I want to prepare properly.

If I can land a contract for masu there,

FOMUS’s financial situation will be more stable.

Even if it’s just once, rental, or subscription,

once they use the masu, they’ll understand the value,

and I can even plan and run the event,

so we can do collaboration events too.

At the event in three days, I want to leave a mark,

and it would be amazing if even one business owner or embassy person finds it interesting.

Also, the new brand I’m planning to launch

might not have come to mind if I had stayed in Ireland.

It came because I spent long hours alone in Greece and Cyprus,

constantly thinking about how to get overseas customers to pick up a masu.

And for the Sagishima project,

I postponed the ceiling art,

and spent the whole day thinking about

what could create the same level of impact and recover the budget.

While walking along the Paphos seaside,

I thought, “A crowdfunding wall of masu could work!!”

and immediately took notes,

then thought about how to make it work.

I’m always doing stuff like this.

To be honest, FOMUS still has a long way to go.

The reality is we haven’t hit anything in Japan or overseas yet.

We’ve scattered seeds everywhere,

but it hasn’t turned into a business that can be harvested.

That’s why from here on,

I need to seriously start making money.

And that means earning foreign currency.

I’ll get big-impact customers,

and also local customers overseas.

I’ll take Japanese people out of the target

and make souvenirs that overseas customers want.

And I’ll find partners who want to enjoy doing this together!!

I thought I had half a year left in Ireland,

but the cost of living is too high,

so I might end it once in March

and move my base to another country.

In other words, in the remaining four months,

I need to expand FOMUS even more in Ireland.

There’s no time, but

I’ll do everything I can now

and produce results.

First, the embassy event on the 21st.

And after that,

I plan to commit to Irish whiskey & Irish music.

These were the two things I was aiming for from the start.

If I can find collaborations and good music,

I can bring it back to Japan,

and a collaboration with Irish whiskey

becomes a huge strength.

There’s so much to do

and so little time,

so I’ll move quickly

and seize as many opportunities as possible!!

So,

I’m sending this serious message

from the plane back to Ireland from Cyprus!
